title: Grace in the Furnace
subtitle: How suffering shapes us into vessels of Christ
author: Jeff Johnson
excerpt: The potter does not despise the cracked clay. He sees in every fracture an opportunity — a place where his hands can press deeper, where the fire can do its refining work.
body: The potter does not despise the cracked clay. He sees in every fracture an opportunity — a place where his hands can press deeper, where the fire can do its refining work. Biblical counseling begins here: not with solutions, but with a theology of the kiln.

Romans 8:28 is often quoted as a comfort verse, deployed quickly in the presence of grief as though the pain were an embarrassment to be expedited. But Paul's argument is richer and more demanding. He does not promise that all things are good; he promises that God works all things together for good to those who are called according to his purpose.

That purpose (prothesis) is not vague. Paul defines it immediately: to be conformed to the image of his Son. Conformity to Christ is the furnace's aim. The counselor's task, then, is not to remove the heat but to help the sufferer understand what the heat is for.

This does not minimize pain. It dignifies it. When we tell a grieving mother that her loss is purposeless randomness, we offer her nothing but a cold universe. When we tell her it is being shaped by a Father who gave his own Son, we give her something to stand on even in the ash.

title: Social Media and the Distracted Soul
subtitle: Reclaiming attention as an act of worship
author: Jeff Johnson
excerpt: Attention is not a neutral faculty. Scripture treats it as a worshipful act. What we behold, we become — and the architecture of social media is designed to fragment beholding into an infinite scroll of competing claimants for the soul.
body: Attention is not a neutral faculty. Scripture treats it as a worshipful act. What we behold, we become — and the architecture of social media is designed to fragment beholding into an infinite scroll of competing claimants for the soul.

Psalm 115 draws the oldest connection between idolatry and transformation: those who make idols become like them. The idol of the social feed is not stone but pixel — yet the dynamic is identical. It promises significance (likes), belonging (followers), and identity (personal brand). These are not trivial desires. They are the desires that only Christ can fill.

Biblical counseling in this space must resist two failures: the moralistic (just stop using it) and the therapeutic (set healthy screen limits). Both treat the behavior without the heart. The deeper question is always: what does this person believe social media can give them that they cannot find in Christ?

The goal is not Luddism. It is liturgical reordering — helping the counselee rebuild a life of attention whose center of gravity is Word, prayer, and embodied community, rather than the dopamine pulse of notification.

title: Come to Me, All Who Labor
subtitle: The yoke of Christ as rest, not burden
author: Jeff Johnson
excerpt: The invitation of Matthew 11:28 is the strangest paradox in the Gospels. Jesus calls the weary to take on a yoke. Rest is found not in the removal of burden but in the exchange of burdens.
body: The invitation of Matthew 11:28 is the strangest paradox in the Gospels. Jesus calls the weary to take on a yoke. Rest is found not in the removal of burden but in the exchange of burdens — from the crushing weight of self-justification to the gentle load of grace.

First-century listeners heard yoke as a rabbinic metaphor for Torah observance and discipleship. Every teacher had a yoke — his interpretive school, his demands, his system. Jesus is not abolishing yokes; he is contrasting his yoke with every other. I am gentle and lowly in heart, he says. No rabbi before him ever described himself that way.

The weariness Jesus addresses is not primarily physical. It is the exhaustion of performing for an audience that is never satisfied. The yoke of Christ is a transfer of jurisdiction: from the court of human opinion to the verdict already spoken at the cross.

In counseling, we meet this weariness constantly. The anxious man who cannot rest because he cannot prove his worth. The perfectionistic woman who serves God as though grace had a co-pay. The gospel addresses not their behavior but their court. Christ has already spoken the verdict: Beloved.

title: The Counselor as Barnabas
subtitle: Encouragement as a theological act
author: Jeff Johnson
excerpt: Barnabas means son of encouragement — yet that title was given, not born. His name was Joseph. The church renamed him for what he did, and what he did was see potential in people others had written off. This is the counselor's vocation.
body: Barnabas means son of encouragement — yet that title was given, not born. His name was Joseph. The church renamed him for what he did, and what he did was see potential in people others had written off. This is the counselor's vocation.

The Greek word paraklesis — encouragement, exhortation, comfort — shares its root with parakletos, the word Jesus uses for the Holy Spirit. This is not accidental. Encouragement, at its depth, is the Spirit's work expressed through human presence. The counselor does not manufacture hope; he mediates it.

Barnabas appears at two critical junctures: he vouches for the feared Saul to a suspicious Jerusalem church, and he later refuses to abandon John Mark when Paul would. In both cases, he extends trust to the person others found disqualifying. This is not naivety. It is a theology of second chances rooted in the God who restored Peter.

Good biblical counseling looks for what God is still doing in a person, not merely cataloging what has broken. It asks: what does redemption look like here? What does resurrection look like, in this particular life, at this particular moment?

title: Anxiety and the Sovereignty of God
subtitle: Why Philippians 4:6 is not a command to simply calm down
author: Jeff Johnson
excerpt: Philippians 4:6 is one of the most quoted and most misapplied verses in pastoral ministry. Do not be anxious about anything is read as a command to stop feeling anxious — as though anxiety were simply a moral failure correctable by willpower.
body: Philippians 4:6 is one of the most quoted and most misapplied verses in pastoral ministry. Do not be anxious about anything is read as a command to stop feeling anxious — as though anxiety were simply a moral failure correctable by willpower. Paul's meaning is richer and more merciful than that.

The word translated be anxious (merimnao) carries the sense of a divided mind — a heart pulled in multiple directions by competing concerns. Paul is not condemning the anxious person; he is diagnosing a condition and prescribing a reorientation: In everything by prayer and supplication with thanksgiving let your requests be made known to God.

Notice the structure: the antidote to divided attention is not focused attention on one's own feelings but directed speech toward God. The anxious person is not told to think less about their problem; they are told to bring the problem to the One who holds all things. Prayer is the transfer of burden.

This is why the passage ends not with your anxiety will disappear but with the peace of God, which surpasses all understanding, will guard your hearts and your minds. The peace comes after the prayer — and it is a peace that outpaces comprehension, meaning the circumstances may not change, but the orientation of the heart does.

title: Rome, Italy
subtitle: Scripture and mission at the heart of the ancient world
author: Jeff Johnson
excerpt: Rome stands as perhaps the most significant city in the entire biblical narrative of the early church. Paul addressed his most theologically comprehensive letter to the church here, writing that the gospel is the power of God for salvation to everyone who believes.
body: ## Biblical & Historical Background

Rome stands as perhaps the most significant city in the entire biblical narrative of the early church. Paul addressed his most theologically comprehensive letter to the church at Rome (Romans 1:7), a congregation he had not yet visited but longed to see. His letter opens with a sweeping proclamation of the gospel as the power of God for salvation to everyone who believes, to the Jew first and also to the Greek (Romans 1:16).

Rome features prominently in the closing chapters of Acts. Paul arrived as a prisoner, yet even his imprisonment advanced the gospel (Philippians 1:12-13). Acts 28 describes how Paul was permitted to live in his own rented dwelling, where he welcomed all who came to him, proclaiming the kingdom of God and teaching about the Lord Jesus Christ with all boldness and without hindrance (Acts 28:30-31). These are the final words of Acts — a powerful conclusion testifying that no earthly power could silence the gospel.
## Gospel & Missions Work

Both Paul and Peter are historically associated with Rome. Paul wrote his prison epistles (Ephesians, Philippians, Colossians, Philemon) from Rome, and tradition holds that both apostles were martyred under Nero's persecution around AD 64-68. Paul was beheaded on the Ostian Way, while Peter was crucified upside down. Second Timothy — Paul's final letter — was written from Roman imprisonment with the solemn awareness that his departure has come (2 Timothy 4:6).

Italy is one of the most significant evangelical mission fields in Western Europe. Despite its deep Christian heritage, less than 1% of Italy's population is evangelical Protestant. The Waldensian Church — a Reformed body with roots stretching back to the 12th century, predating the Protestant Reformation by three hundred years — represents the oldest continuously existing Protestant witness on Italian soil. Campus ministry organizations including IFES and The Navigators maintain work among Rome's large student population, providing gospel witness in the intellectual heart of the city where Paul once proclaimed Christ with all boldness and without hindrance.

title: Naples, Italy
subtitle: Where Paul disembarked and the gospel preceded him
author: Jeff Johnson
excerpt: While Naples itself is not mentioned by name in Scripture, the region directly figures into Paul's final journey to Rome. Acts 28:13 records that Paul's ship sailed to Puteoli in the Bay of Naples, where he found brothers already there and was invited to stay seven days. The gospel had preceded the Apostle.
body: ## Biblical & Historical Background

While Naples itself is not mentioned by name in Scripture, the region directly figures into Paul's final journey to Rome. Acts 28:13 records that Paul's ship sailed from Rhegium to Puteoli — the ancient Roman port in the Bay of Naples, the principal harbor for grain ships from Alexandria. It was here that Paul disembarked for the final overland leg to Rome.

Remarkably, Acts 28:14 records that Paul found brothers there and was invited to stay with them for seven days. This brief note reveals that an established Christian community already existed in the Bay of Naples region before Paul arrived — likely carried there by the constant maritime traffic between Rome, Alexandria, and the eastern Mediterranean. The Spirit of God does not wait for apostles to move first; He goes ahead.
## Gospel & Missions Work

Naples is often cited as one of the most spiritually needy cities in Western Europe. Deep poverty, generations of social dysfunction tied to organized crime, and a pervasive sense of fatalism have shaped a population that is both hungry for hope and resistant to change. The gospel of grace — that a person can be fully forgiven, genuinely transformed, and given a living hope through faith in Christ — speaks directly into this context.

Several evangelical churches in the city have developed significant social ministries — working with drug addicts, the homeless, and families trapped in cycles of poverty — as tangible expressions of the gospel's transforming power in one of Europe's most challenged urban environments.

title: Crete (Chania), Greece
subtitle: Titus, elders in every town, and the power of the gospel to transform
author: Jeff Johnson
excerpt: Crete appears at several pivotal moments in the New Testament — from Paul's storm-tossed voyage past its shores to his commission to Titus to appoint elders in every town. Cretans were among the very first recipients of the Holy Spirit at Pentecost, likely carrying the gospel home before Paul ever arrived.
body: ## Biblical & Historical Background

Crete appears at several pivotal moments in the New Testament. Most dramatically, Acts 27 records Paul's fateful voyage past the island. Sailing under Roman guard toward Rome, the ship passed along the southern coast of Crete, putting in at Fair Havens. Paul warned the centurion against continuing the voyage, but his warning was ignored. After 14 days adrift in the Euroclydon, they were shipwrecked at Malta. The episode powerfully illustrates both Paul's spiritual discernment and the sovereign providence of God in preserving his servant for the mission ahead.

Crete is also directly addressed in the New Testament epistles. Paul left his trusted coworker Titus on the island with specific instructions: This is why I left you in Crete, so that you might put what remained into order, and appoint elders in every town as I directed you (Titus 1:5). The letter to Titus provides some of the most practical instruction in all of Paul's writings on church governance, elder qualifications, and sound doctrine.
## Gospel & Missions Work

Paul's frank quotation of the Cretan poet Epimenides in Titus 1:12 reflects both the cultural reputation of the island and the transforming power of the gospel: Christ saves people out of exactly these patterns and forms communities of truth, self-control, and diligence where none existed before. Cretans were also present in Jerusalem on the Day of Pentecost (Acts 2:11), meaning converts from Crete were among the very first recipients of the Holy Spirit.

The Greek Evangelical Church — a Reformed denomination with Presbyterian polity — maintains congregations on Crete, representing the direct fruit of 19th-century Reformed missionary investment. Greek-language media ministry through Trans World Radio and other organizations carries the gospel of grace into homes across the island that Titus was commissioned to evangelize nearly two thousand years ago.

title: Ephesus, Turkey
subtitle: The hub of Paul's greatest missionary effort and the church that lost its first love
author: Jeff Johnson
excerpt: Ephesus is arguably the most extensively documented city in the entire New Testament outside of Jerusalem. Paul spent approximately three years here — longer than anywhere else recorded in Acts — and from this hub all the residents of Asia heard the word of the Lord, both Jews and Greeks.
body: ## Biblical & Historical Background

Ephesus is arguably the most extensively documented city in the entire New Testament outside of Jerusalem. Paul spent approximately three years here — longer than anywhere else recorded in Acts. From his two-year teaching ministry in the hall of Tyrannus, all the residents of Asia heard the word of the Lord, both Jews and Greeks (Acts 19:10). The temple of Artemis was one of the Seven Wonders of the Ancient World, and Paul's gospel directly threatened the idol-making economy — a reminder that the gospel always has economic implications when it confronts false religion.

Paul's letter to the Ephesians sets forth one of the most exalted theological visions in all of Scripture: the eternal purpose of God to unite all things in Christ (Ephesians 1:10), salvation by grace through faith not of works, so that no one may boast (Ephesians 2:8-9), and the full armor of God for spiritual warfare (Ephesians 6:10-18).
## Gospel & Missions Work

Paul's farewell address to the Ephesian elders at Miletus (Acts 20:17-38) is among the most moving passages in Acts, and his warning about fierce wolves arising from within the church proved tragically prescient. Ephesus is also one of the Seven Churches of Revelation (Revelation 2:1-7), commended for doctrinal discernment but rebuked for having abandoned its first love. The risen Christ's warning that He would remove their lampstand unless they repented is a solemn reminder to every generation that the gospel must be held fast, not merely inherited.

Ephesus today lies in Turkey, a nation that is approximately 99% Muslim. The ABCFM with strong Presbyterian participation sent missionaries to Anatolia beginning in the early 19th century, establishing schools, hospitals, and churches throughout the region. The International Mission Board (SBC) has supported church-planting among the Turkish people, one of the largest unreached people groups in the world. Both Baptist and Presbyterian mission represent a sustained, if costly, commitment to the region where Paul once declared that all of Asia heard the word of the Lord.

title: Mykonos, Greece
subtitle: Where the nations gather and the gospel still needs to be heard
author: Jeff Johnson
excerpt: Mykonos does not appear by name in Scripture, but it lies in the heart of the Aegean Sea through which Paul repeatedly sailed. Every island in this chain was part of the world Christ was reclaiming through the proclamation of His apostles.
body: ## Biblical & Historical Background

Mykonos does not appear by name in Scripture, but it lies in the heart of the Aegean Sea through which Paul repeatedly sailed. The island sits just a few miles northeast of Delos — one of the most important religious centers of the ancient Greek world, the supposed birthplace of Apollo and Artemis, its massive sanctuary complex drawing pilgrims from across the Mediterranean.

Acts 20:15 records that on his final journey to Jerusalem, Paul's vessel passed opposite Chios and then touched at Samos — a route passing directly through the waters surrounding Mykonos and Delos. The proximity of Delos, with its dense concentration of idol temples and pagan worship, to the sea lanes Paul traveled is a reminder that the gospel moved through a world saturated with false religion — not unlike our own.
## Gospel & Missions Work

Mykonos is one of the world's most internationally recognized tourist destinations, drawing millions of visitors annually. This extraordinary concentration of people from virtually every nation creates a unique mission opportunity: the nations, in a sense, come to this small island.

For the Baptist or Presbyterian visitor to Mykonos, the opportunity is primarily one of personal witness — conversations with locals and fellow travelers that the extraordinary setting naturally invites. The island's beauty is itself a natural opening for conversations about the Creator and about the Christ who is Lord of every sea and every shore. Every believer visiting Mykonos carries the same commission Paul carried past these waters: to bear witness to the One in whom we live and move and have our being.

title: Athens, Greece
subtitle: Mars Hill, the unknown God, and the gospel in the world's intellectual capital
author: Jeff Johnson
excerpt: Athens occupies a uniquely prominent place in Paul's missionary narrative as the intellectual and cultural capital of the ancient world. Paul's address on the Areopagus is a masterpiece of contextual apologetics — beginning with their altar to the unknown God and ending with the proclamation of resurrection.
body: ## Biblical & Historical Background

Athens occupies a uniquely prominent place in Paul's missionary narrative as the intellectual and cultural capital of the ancient world. Acts 17:16 records that Paul's spirit was provoked within him as he saw that the city was full of idols. He reasoned in the synagogue and in the marketplace daily, drawing the attention of Epicurean and Stoic philosophers who brought him before the Areopagus — the ancient council that met on Mars Hill.

Paul's address on the Areopagus is a masterpiece of contextual apologetics. He begins with careful observation, noting the altar inscribed To the unknown god, then moves from their own poetic traditions — In him we live and move and have our being — to the proclamation of the one Creator God, the appointed day of judgment, and the resurrection of Jesus.
## Gospel & Missions Work

Paul's method is instructive: he found genuine points of contact in the culture without compromising the gospel's distinctive claims. He did not soften the offense of the resurrection — the very point at which some in his audience mocked — because it was non-negotiable. Real conversions followed, including Dionysius the Areopagite and a woman named Damaris (Acts 17:34). The gospel divides and saves even in the most sophisticated intellectual settings.

Athens is one of the most active evangelical mission fields in Greece. The Greek Evangelical Church has its headquarters in Athens and maintains several congregations in the city. Perhaps the most remarkable recent development is the growth of gospel witness among Iranian, Afghan, and Syrian refugees — many proving extraordinarily receptive to the gospel — creating thriving new congregations that are reshaping the evangelical landscape. Paul stood on Mars Hill and declared the unknown God. That same proclamation is now reaching people from across the world who have arrived at Athens' shores.

title: Santorini, Greece
subtitle: Beauty, created glory, and the gospel on the edge of the Aegean
author: Jeff Johnson
excerpt: Santorini — known in antiquity as Thera — does not appear by name in the biblical text, yet it lay squarely within the Aegean maritime world of the New Testament church. The apostolic mission pressed through every natural barrier, and no island in the Aegean lay beyond the reach of the gospel's advance.
body: ## Biblical & Historical Background

Santorini — known in antiquity as Thera — does not appear by name in the biblical text, yet it lay squarely within the Aegean maritime world of the New Testament church. The island sits approximately 120 miles southeast of Athens and was a known waypoint in ancient Mediterranean navigation. Its dramatic caldera — the remnant of one of the most catastrophic volcanic eruptions in human history — would have been a well-known geographical feature to ancient sailors.

Thera lay along the commercial and naval routes connecting mainland Greece to Crete, Rhodes, and the eastern Mediterranean — routes traversed repeatedly by Paul and other early missionaries. The sea itself — often violent and unpredictable, as Paul knew firsthand from his shipwreck off Malta — was both obstacle and highway in the spread of the kingdom of God. The apostolic mission pressed through every natural barrier, and no island in the Aegean lay beyond the reach of the gospel's advance.
## Gospel & Missions Work

Santorini today is one of the world's most iconic tourist destinations, drawing well over two million visitors annually, attracting people from virtually every nation on earth. This creates one of the most internationally diverse mission contexts imaginable — compressed onto a small volcanic island in the Aegean Sea.

For the visitor to Santorini, the primary opportunity is personal witness. The created beauty of Santorini — the caldera, the light, the sea — is itself a powerful witness to the Creator and a natural opening for gospel conversation. Presbyterian mission investment in the Greek Evangelical Church and Baptist mission through the IMB have both contributed to broader Greek evangelical infrastructure that reaches island communities like Santorini even in the absence of a resident congregation or missionary.

title: Bar, Montenegro
subtitle: Illyricum — the edge of Paul's apostolic vision
author: Jeff Johnson
excerpt: Bar lies in the ancient region of Illyricum — and Paul's own summary of his missionary career places it at the boundary: from Jerusalem and all the way around to Illyricum I have fulfilled the ministry of the gospel of Christ.
body: ## Biblical & Historical Background

Bar (ancient Antibari) lies on the eastern Adriatic coast of modern Montenegro, situated in the ancient region of Illyricum. While Bar itself does not appear in Scripture, its region is directly referenced by the Apostle Paul in one of the most remarkable summary statements of his missionary career. In Romans 15:19, Paul writes: from Jerusalem and all the way around to Illyricum I have fulfilled the ministry of the gospel of Christ. This sweeping geographical statement marks Illyricum as the northwestern boundary of Paul's completed apostolic theater.

The region also appears in Paul's final correspondence: Titus has gone to Dalmatia (2 Timothy 4:10) — the southern Adriatic coast that includes the region near Bar — indicating that active apostolic mission work was being carried out on this coastline during Paul's final years. Paul's stated theological drive was always to press toward regions where Christ had not been named, so that I would not build on someone else's foundation (Romans 15:20). The Illyrian coast represented exactly this kind of frontier.
## Gospel & Missions Work

Montenegro today represents one of the smallest and least-reached evangelical mission fields in Europe. The country's population of roughly 620,000 is predominantly Eastern Orthodox, with a significant Muslim minority, and in both communities religious identity functions more as ethnic and cultural belonging than as personal, saving faith. The biblical gospel of grace through faith in Christ alone is largely unknown in the majority of the population.

A handful of evangelical congregations in Podgorica and other Montenegrin cities — supported by both Reformed and Baptist mission investment — represent the current evangelical witness in this profoundly underserved country. Bar and the Montenegrin coastline remain a place where the gospel needs to be carried afresh, honoring the apostolic mandate to go where Christ has not yet been named.

title: Corfu, Greece
subtitle: Gateway to the Adriatic and the apostolic corridor westward
author: Jeff Johnson
excerpt: Corfu (ancient Corcyra) sits on the maritime route between the eastern Mediterranean and the Adriatic Sea — directly on the sea lanes Paul would have used in his journeys to and from Rome. It was the gateway to the Adriatic in ancient navigation.
body: ## Biblical & Historical Background

Corfu (ancient Corcyra) is the largest of the Ionian Islands, located just off the northwestern coast of Greece near the Albanian border. It sits on the maritime route between the eastern Mediterranean and the Adriatic Sea — a critically important passage for ancient ships traveling between the Aegean and Italy, and therefore directly on the sea lanes Paul would have used in his journeys to and from Rome.

Paul's journey to Rome (Acts 27-28), his subsequent release and further travels, and the extension of apostolic mission toward Illyricum and Dalmatia all placed Corfu squarely within the geographical corridor of the early church's westward gospel advance. The Ionian Islands were part of the same Greek-speaking world Paul addressed throughout his ministry.
## Gospel & Missions Work

Corfu has a small but notable evangelical history, partly rooted in the period of British protectorate rule (1814-1864), when Anglican worship was formally established on the island. The Greek Evangelical Church — the Reformed denomination with Presbyterian polity — has had a presence in the Ionian Islands as part of its broader national network.

Albania, just across the narrow channel from Corfu, is one of the most remarkable evangelical mission stories in Europe. After decades of enforced state atheism, Albania has seen extraordinary evangelical church growth since 1991, with both Baptist and Presbyterian/Reformed mission bodies playing significant roles. Southern Baptist missionaries were among the first to enter Albania after the fall of communism, establishing Baptist churches that grew rapidly. The proximity of Corfu to this flourishing Albanian evangelical church creates natural opportunities for cross-border fellowship and collaborative mission.

title: Sicily (Messina), Italy
subtitle: Paul ashore at Syracuse and the gospel at the crossroads of the Mediterranean
author: Jeff Johnson
excerpt: Sicily appears directly in the narrative of Paul's journey to Rome. Acts 28:12 records: And landing at Syracuse, we stayed there for three days. Syracuse was the largest and most important city of ancient Sicily, and Paul's three-day stop there is explicitly recorded by Luke.
body: ## Biblical & Historical Background

Sicily appears directly in the narrative of Paul's journey to Rome. Acts 28:12 records: And landing at Syracuse, we stayed there for three days. Syracuse — on the southeastern coast of Sicily — was the largest and most important city of ancient Sicily. Though the passage gives no details of evangelistic activity, Paul's consistent pattern throughout Acts was to proclaim the gospel wherever he found himself.

From Syracuse, Acts 28:13 records that the ship made a circuit and arrived at Rhegium — the toe of the Italian boot directly across the narrow Strait of Messina from Sicily. Messina sits at the very northern tip of Sicily on this strait, making it the point geographically closest to Paul's passage. The Strait of Messina — barely two miles wide at its narrowest — was one of the most strategically important and navigationally treacherous passages in the ancient Mediterranean.
## Gospel & Missions Work

Sicily presents a genuinely active and growing evangelical mission field within the broader context of southern Italy. The Waldensian Church — Italy's historic Reformed denomination — has maintained a presence in Sicily as part of its national network. The Italian Baptist Union has likewise maintained congregations and church-planting efforts across Sicily, and the International Mission Board (SBC) has partnered with Italian Baptist work in the region.

The island's history of deep poverty and social hardship has created a population genuinely open to the hope the gospel provides. Evangelical churches in Sicily — Baptist, Reformed, and Brethren — often function as genuine communities of grace where people from broken backgrounds encounter forgiveness, belonging, and transformed lives. African-led evangelical congregations have also become significant witnesses, many arriving as migrants and bringing with them a vibrant faith making a tangible impact alongside the historic Baptist and Reformed witness in the place where Paul first stepped ashore en route to Rome.

title: Barcelona, Spain
subtitle: The end of Paul's apostolic ambition and the gospel coming full circle
author: Jeff Johnson
excerpt: Barcelona and the broader region of Hispania hold a unique place in Pauline missiology as the stated ultimate destination of his westward apostolic ambition. I hope to see you in passing as I go to Spain, Paul writes in Romans 15:24 — Spain representing the western edge of the known Roman world.
body: ## Biblical & Historical Background

Barcelona and the broader region of Hispania hold a unique place in Pauline missiology as the stated ultimate destination of his westward apostolic ambition. In Romans 15:24, Paul writes: I hope to see you in passing as I go to Spain. Spain represented the western edge of the known Roman world — the Pillars of Hercules (modern Gibraltar) marked the boundary of the Mediterranean — and Paul's burning desire to preach not where Christ has already been named (Romans 15:20) drove him to envision mission to the uttermost geographical reaches of the empire.

Whether Paul ever reached Spain is one of the most discussed questions in New Testament scholarship. Clement of Rome, writing around AD 96, states that Paul reached the farthest bounds of the West — widely interpreted as Spain. The Muratorian Fragment (c. AD 170) explicitly states the journey of Paul from the city to Spain. The weight of early testimony favors Paul having fulfilled this ambition, making Barcelona (ancient Barcino) a plausible destination in the apostolic mission to Hispania.
## Gospel & Missions Work

Paul's Spain ambition reflects a missionary theology in which no people, no city, no region is beyond the reach of the gospel — and in which the church is obligated to press toward the unreached. This is the theology of Romans 10:14-15: How are they to hear without someone preaching? And how are they to preach unless they are sent?

Spain is one of the most encouraging evangelical mission stories in contemporary Europe. For centuries, evangelical Christianity was violently suppressed under the Inquisition. Religious freedom only arrived after 1975, and in the decades since, evangelical Christianity has grown remarkably. Latin American evangelical immigrants — from Baptist and Reformed church backgrounds in Colombia, Venezuela, Mexico, and beyond — have been a remarkable catalyst for evangelical growth in Barcelona, reversing the direction of mission: the fruit of centuries of gospel work in the Americas is now returning to evangelize the mother country. Paul longed to go to Spain. That longing is being fulfilled today in Barcelona.
